title: practical english usage - Part 74
content:
after a noun or be (►   106).

Sometimes active and passive infinitives can have similar meanings, especially That window must be repaired before tonight.

There's a lot of work to be done. She ought to be told about it.

Passive infinitives have the same kind of meaning as other passive forms (►   57).

3  passive      infinitive:      (to)  be   + past    participle not happen, or that may not have happened (►  90).

We   often use perfect infinitives to talk about ’ unreal’  past events: things that did I'm sorry not to have come on Thursday. (= ... that I didn’ t come  . ..) (= It’ s nice that I have finished ...) It's nice to have finished work for the day.

past tenses (►  44-45).

Perfect infinitives can have the same kind of meaning as perfect tenses (►  6) or 2 perfect      infinitive:      (to)  have      + past    participle Why's she so late? She can't still be working.

This time tomorrow I'll be lying on the beach. (future progressive tense: ►    41 ) I noticed that he seemed to be smoking a lot.

It's nice to be sitting here with you.

are / were / will be continuing around the time that we  are talking about.

Like other progressive forms (►  3), progressive infinitives suggest that situations 1  progressive         infinitive:      (to)  be   . . .ing (e.g. (to) be written).

be writing), perfect infinitives (e.g. (to) have written), and passive infinitives to, ►    91. Besides simple infinitives, there are also progressive infinitives (e.g. (to) Infinitives are most often used with to (e.g. to go, to start). For infinitives without 89 infinitives:      forms For full details of the uses of infinitives, see the following sections.

He came to London to look for work.

•    to express a person’ s purpose (►   112) You have the right to remain silent.

I don't want to talk. I'm anxious to contact your brother.

•    as the object or complement of a verb, adjective or noun (►   97-98,  101-102) The main thing is to relax. It's nice to talk to you.

To watch him  eating really gets on my nerves.

•    as the subject or complement of a clause (►  92) An infinitive can also be used, alone or with other words: Do you think she's ready? We must  get some more light bulbs.

or a modal auxiliary verb as part of a verb phrase.

Infinitives have many functions. An infinitive can be used, for example, after do 88  infinitives:      introduction grammar •  90  use of perfect infinitives: glad to have left I would like to have seen Harry's face when Nadia walked in.

He was to have been the new ambassador, but he fell ill.

I meant to have telephoned, but I forgot. (   I meant to telephone. ..) 'unreal’  past situations that are the opposite of what really happened.

After some verbs (e.g. mean, be, would like), perfect infinitives can refer to 2 perfect      infinitive       for   'unreal'    past finished . ..) We hope to have finished the job by next Saturday. (= ... that we  will have She was sorry to have missed James. (= ... that she had missed James.) I'm glad to have left school at last. (= I’ m  glad that I have left...) meaning as perfect or past tenses.

Perfect infinitives (to have gone, to have left, etc) can have the same kind of 1  perfect      or  past    meaning 90 use   of  perfect      infinitives:      glad     to   have      left He began slowly to get up off the floor.

putting the adverb in another position.

not true, but people who  believe in the rule try to avoid split infinitives by style. There is an old rule which  says that split infinitives are incorrect. This is Split infinitive structures are quite common in English, especially in an informal He began to slowly get up off the floor.

I'd like to really understand philosophy.

infinitive by an adverb.

A  'split infinitive’  is a structure in which  to is separated from the rest of the 7 split    infinitive:      to   really      understand For the use of to instead of a whole infinitive (e.g. I'd like to), ►    280.

After the preposition to we  use -ing forms (►   104.2).

Note that the infinitive marker to (as in He wanted to go) is not a preposition.
